I'm a loving mother of two awesome teenagers (daughter just graduated in top 10 of her high school class and off to college for engineering!). I had an abortion after my 2nd child. There - I said it in public for the first time. It was a wanted pregnancy for sure, yet for reasons I won't delve into here I opted to terminate. It was a VERY difficult period. Yet, that doesn't mean that it equals being the wrong choice. I'm so, so grateful for the OB/gyn female doctor I worked with. I've had a few episodes of sadness about it but I truly believe this was what was right for me, us. Really, this whole issue boils down to whether a WOMAN has autonomy and rights over her OWN body. My case was even more painful because I ended up having to terminate later than I would have liked due to the timing of certain diagnostic procedures. The whole thing is kind of a blur now but I remember these stretches of just waiting. For example, they weren't able to do the first test on me because my cervical opening was so constricted (2 prior pregnancies were c-sections) and the doctor refused to continue. So, then I had to wait for the next acceptable time frame for whatever the next diagnostic procedure was. It was agonizing. My point is that I had a later term abortion than I wanted yet it was kind of out of my control. Seems no one really talks about that. Thank you Liz for allowing me a place where I feel relatively safe bringing this up. Thank you SO much for all of your research you present about this issue!

I’m actually a Constitutional law junkie (if I hadn’t become a nurse, I would have become a lawyer). 

There’s a couple of comments that I would make. First of all, the parents and kids analogy isn’t 100% accurate. The Constitution isn’t about the powers of the federal government as much as it is (or is supposed to be) a check on the powers of the federal government. Many of these checks have been eroded due to the actions of Woodrow Wilson and FDR and then just a continued slow erosion since then. 

Returning abortion laws to the states is just one thing that would start working towards returning the United States back to its original form of a Constitutional republic. The more local to you government decisions are made, the more power that you, as a citizen, have. Because of this, all decision making should be done at the the most local practical level of government.

This is an opportunity and a call for individual citizens to become more active in government (and stop sleeping through civics. Stop it. Civics is IMPORTANT!) It doesn’t matter if you have kids or not, make sure you know who is on your local school board and what kids are learning. Make sure you know your city council members. All levels of city, county, and state government is more accessible to you, as a citizen, more responsible and accountable to you and should therefore be the levels of government where things are managed unless they are powers specifically granted to the federal government by the constitution.

I’m a huge proponent of slashing everything from the federal government that isn’t an enumerated power and returning ALL of it to the states. Yes, there would be adjustment pains but in the long run you would see a more effective, more accountable to the citizens government… more involved and aware citizens, and a leaner, less bloated and FAR less costly federal government. 

It would also cut down on a LOT of animosity. POTUS is NOT supposed to be “king of the world” and by returning the federal government to its constitutionally enumerated powers, we strip that role back down to an appropriate level of influence and importance.
